title: practical english usage - Part 159
content:
Thank you very much  indeed. That is most kind of you. (     ...  That is and disapproval (including one-syllable adjectives) to mean ‘ very’ .

In a rather formal style, most can be used with adjectives expressing approval not He's more lazy than stupid, (     He^sdazier than stupid.) than another), we  use more; comparatives with -er are not possible.

When  we  compare two descriptions (saying that one is more suitable or accurate or The road's getting more and more steep, (   ... steeper and steeper.) by than; forms with -er are also possible.

This can happen, for example, when a comparative is not followed immediately Sometimes more!most are used with adjectives that normally have -er/-est.

5  more,      most      with    short    adjectives grammar •  206  using comparatives and superlatives originally a form of the demonstrative pronoun, meaning 'by that much'.

Note that in this structure, the word the is not really the definite article - it was ‘ How do you like your coffee? ‘ The stronger the better.' in sentences ending the better.

A  short form of this structure is used in the expression The more the merrier, and The more information that comes in, the more confused the picture is.

In longer structures, that is sometimes used before the first verb.

The more money he makes, the more useless things he buys.

Noun phrases with more can be used in this structure.

The more I study, the less I learn.

The more dangerous it is, the more I like it. (     T-he-more it not The older I get, the happier I am. (     Older I-get, more I am happy.) the + comparative expression + subject + verb Word  order (in both clauses): together.

We   can use comparatives with the... the... to say that things change or vary 5 the    ...  the    .., not We're going more and more slowly, (     ... more slowly and more slowly?) I'm getting fatter and fatter.

We   can use double comparatives to say that something is changing.

4 double      comparatives:         fatter       and     fatter;      more      and     more      slowly Some people feel that a superlative is incorrect in this case.

I'll give you the bigger/biggest steak: I'm not very hungry.

I like Lily and Mia, but I think Mia's the nicer/nicest of the two.

of a superlative.

When  a group only has two members, we  sometimes use a comparative instead 3 groups      with    two    members He's the best in the team.

- He plays better than everybody else in the team.

Your accent is the worst in the class, (     .. . the worse...) - Your accent is worse than mine.

Emily's the tallest of the four girls, (     .. .-the-taUer-. ..) - Emily's taller than her three sisters.

grammar •  207  much  older, by far the oldest, etc (in 11  ndi  ..

. a bil utt er shop*. ..) There are much/far nicer shops in the town centre.

normally used to modify comparatives before nouns.

meaning 'recovered from an illness' (►   402.1). Any, no, a bit and a lot are not Quite cannot be used with comparatives except in the expression quite better, is your mother any better?  Your cooking is even worse than Harry's.

a lot happier (informal)                 a little less expensive a bit more sensible (informal)        She looks no older than her daughter, very much  nicer                             rather more quickly Russian is much/far more difficult than Spanish.

My boyfriend is much/far older than me. (     .. .very older-than me.) a bit (informal), and even.

very much,  a lot (informal), lots (informal), any and no (►  373), rather, a little, We   cannot use very with comparatives. Instead, we  use, for example, much,  far, 1  much,      far,    etc   with    comparatives 207much      older,      by   far    the    oldest,       etc For tenses after than, ►    231 .       For the first/second/best, etc + present/past perfect, ►    56.

You're more stubborn than anybody I know.

It's the best book I've ever read. This is my hardest job yet.

and superlatives.

'Non-affirmative' words like ever, yet and any (►  222) often follow comparatives 8  than      anybody;         the    best     ...  ever He thinks he's the world's strongest man.
